paragraphs.text: October 2025

section_path: Reply to Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: EU Transparency Number: 167661034032-04

section_path: Reply to Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: The Face-value European Alliance for Ticketing (FEAT), representing independent music promoters, venues, and sports clubs from nine EU Member States, welcomes the opportunity to provide feedback on the EU's Digital Fairness Act.

section_path: Reply to Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: Our members are the glue that makes live events possible. They bring together artists, athletes, and audiences by managing every aspect of event delivery – from booking venues and budgeting to marketing, ticketing, and logistics. They take on the full risk and responsibility of staging live events, relying on ticket sales to make their work sustainable.

section_path: Reply to Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: unauthorised ‘resale’ of event tickets at extortionate prices.

paragraphs.text: Tens of thousands of consumers across the EU are exploited by ticket scalpers each year – commercial operators who profit from the unauthorised ‘resale’ of event tickets at extortionate prices. Websites enable this business illicitly, without authorisation from the event organiser. This means they cannot reissue official tickets that guarantee entry to the consumer. Offers that appear on these illicit websites are likely fraudulent.

section_path: Reply to Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: The only way to achieve this is through authorised channels that can verify and reissue tickets officially and at their intended price.
Just as the unauthorised resale of plane, train, or hotel bookings would be seen as unacceptable, fans deserve the same guarantee of authenticity for live event tickets.

paragraphs.text: This situation is leading to a loss of trust in live event ticketing. Consumers need certainty that the ticket they buy will grant them entry. The only way to achieve this is through authorised channels that can verify and reissue tickets officially and at their intended price. Just as the unauthorised resale of plane, train, or hotel bookings would be seen as unacceptable, fans deserve the same guarantee of authenticity for live event tickets.

section_path: Reply to Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: When a ticket is resold on TicketSwap (at face value), the primary agent that issued the original ticket is notified of the resale, and a brand new ticket is reissued to the new buyer.

paragraphs.text: As an example of how ticket resale can work well, Belgian promoters have successfully integrated their primary ticketing agents with the third-party website, TicketSwap. When a ticket is resold on TicketSwap (at face value), the primary agent that issued the original ticket is notified of the resale, and a brand new ticket is reissued to the new buyer. This is a simple solution that gives consumers the confidence to know their ticket, officially resold, will guarantee them entry.

section_path: Reply to Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: A step improvement in enforcement of EU and Member State legislation

evidence_quotes.quote: tickets resold at face value

paragraphs.text: Work with the live performance sector so that event ticket resale quickly moves to a promoter-authorised only system, with tickets resold at face value

evidence_quotes.quote: don't guarantee entry

paragraphs.text: Each year, FEAT's members host thousands of events across Europe. Many of these will see hundreds of fans arriving with tickets bought from unauthorised 'resale' websites that don't guarantee entry.

evidence_quotes.quote: violate the event organiser's terms and conditions

paragraphs.text: 'Resale' offers often appear before the official sale begins, or otherwise violate the event organiser's terms and conditions or national and EU law. Many involve fraudulent or duplicated tickets, and some are never delivered because they were listed speculatively – where tickets are listed on unauthorised websites before they have been acquired from the official provider. As these websites are not connected to official ticketing systems, none of the tickets they 'resell' can guarantee entry. Fans are often distraught when they discover their tickets are invalid, particularly after spending significant sums on travel and accommodation.

evidence_quotes.quote: Consumers routinely pay several times the face value for these illicitly resold tickets.

paragraphs.text: Consumers routinely pay several times the face value for these illicitly resold tickets. Research carried out by FEAT in August 2025 found:

evidence_quotes.quote: priced up to €5,000 (face value €100–€250).

paragraphs.text: Over 500 tickets listed for a Kendrick Lamar concert in Stockholm (9th Aug 2025), priced up to €5,000 (face value €100–€250).

evidence_quotes.quote: 40% priced above €1,000

paragraphs.text: Over 80 tickets listed for Lady Gaga in Barcelona (28th/29th/31st Oct 2025), with 40% priced above €1,000 (face value €86–€171).

evidence_quotes.quote: two and four times the original ticket price

paragraphs.text: Over 200 tickets listed for Hans Zimmer in Amsterdam (27th & 28th Feb 2026), listed on average between two and four times the original ticket price (face value €93 and €203).

FEAT estimates Europe's unauthorised resale market to exceed €2.5bn annually, with StubHub Holdings (created from the merger of the world's two largest unauthorised 'resale' websites) valuing the non-US market potential at €20bn over the medium term. Most offers on these websites are made by illicit traders – also known as 'scalpers' – the top 10% of whom account for over 70% of gross ticket sales.

evidence_quotes.quote: Offers for Rafael Nadal's final match reached €20,000 on Viagogo,
regularly sell at extreme mark-ups.

paragraphs.text: Similar patterns appear in sport. Offers for Rafael Nadal's final match reached €20,000 on Viagogo, while tickets for the annual Barcelona versus Real Madrid 'El Clásico' regularly sell at extreme mark-ups. And, in June 2025, Spanish former women's tennis world #1 Garbiñe Muguruza paid for supposed 'resale' tickets on StubHub to see Bruce Springsteen, which never arrived.

Over the past few years, music tourism in Europe has seen a significant increase, with the resale of tickets becoming increasingly borderless. Viagogo's own statistics suggest it will double by 2032, as fans increasingly travel across borders to see global acts like Taylor Swift, Coldplay and Dua Lipa. A 2024 survey on the main travel purpose of Europeans also found that more respondents were planning to travel for a music or sporting event than for business.

Furthermore, many traders operate outside the EU and avoid taxation. A recent German legal case found a single trader owing €2 million in unpaid taxes from the commercial resale of tickets to concerts and sporting events.

evidence_quotes.quote: In some cases, ticket scalping can have dangerous consequences.
speculative resale can pose safety risks
Viagogo and StubHub allowed traders to sell hundreds of tickets that they were then unable to fulfil.

paragraphs.text: In some cases, ticket scalping can have dangerous consequences. The 2019 Champions League final in Madrid showed how speculative resale can pose safety risks, after Viagogo and StubHub allowed traders to sell hundreds of tickets that they were then unable to fulfil. The Madrid offices of StubHub and Viagogo had to be closed to protect staff, with some incidents of violence that broke out before the game attributed to ticketless, frustrated fans gathering en masse in the city centre. There are many other similar examples. In a 2022 Europa League quarterfinal, FC Barcelona saw 30,000 Eintracht Frankfurt supporters end up inside their stadium despite an official allocation of only 5,000 tickets for the away team – prompting security concerns.

evidence_quotes.quote: fake tickets sold by resale websites were noted as a contributing factor to a fatal crush at a football match in El Salvador.

paragraphs.text: In 2023, fake tickets sold by resale websites were noted as a contributing factor to a fatal crush at a football match in El Salvador. Without scaremongering, if nothing is done, the day will surely come when there are serious injuries or worse within the EU.

evidence_quotes.quote: illicit traders deceiving consumers by falsely presenting that they have tickets to sell when the event organiser has not yet issued any.

paragraphs.text: A range of horizontal EU and national laws apply in this area. These include the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive, which prohibits traders from providing false or misleading information about a product. This would likely include illicit traders deceiving consumers by falsely presenting that they have tickets to sell when the event organiser has not yet issued any.

evidence_quotes.quote: the event organiser's terms and conditions are enforceable

paragraphs.text: In a 2022 ruling, the European Court of Justice (ECJ) confirmed that the relationship between a live event organiser and a ticket-buyer constitutes a service contract. This means that the event organiser's terms and conditions are enforceable, and confirms that live event tickets are no different to airline, hotel, or train tickets – none of which can be resold on the open market.

evidence_quotes.quote: ban on resale above face value for designated venues and events.

paragraphs.text: As well as contract law, other horizontal national laws may apply, including property law and, in instances of fraud, criminal law. In addition, some Member States have passed laws that specifically govern ticket resale. In France, resale without authorisation from an event organiser is a criminal offence, resulting in fines between €15,000 and €30,000. Belgium has similar rules on authorised resale, but fans can resell 'occasionally and not for profit' if they are unable to attend at the last minute. And, in Ireland, this takes the form of a ban on resale above face value for designated venues and events. Four other Member States have variations of these laws.

evidence_quotes.quote: unauthorised resale websites.
They host resale offers that breach national or EU law, and continue to advertise them aggressively via search engines.

paragraphs.text: A weak enforcement regime across the EU has served to protect unauthorised resale websites. They host resale offers that breach national or EU law, and continue to advertise them aggressively via search engines.

evidence_quotes.quote: unauthorised websites allow traders to return to publishing illicit offers within days or weeks.

paragraphs.text: Despite court action only being an option for organisations with deep pockets or strong unions, there have been almost 60 major legal ticket resale cases in recent years. Judges have issued minimal fines for offences long after the concerts have taken place, and even when promoters are successful, unauthorised websites allow traders to return to publishing illicit offers within days or weeks. This forces event organisers back to court to prove the original order has been violated. Independent European promoters do not have the money or extensive legal teams of multinational companies like Viagogo, and are unable to compete.
The Digital Services Act (DSA) promised an efficient process for reporting illegal ticket offers, which FEAT wholeheartedly welcomes.

However, our experience with the DSA so far has been severely lacking. FEAT's members have reported 296 individual resale offers, representing nearly 1,000 tickets. Of these reports, just one has been responded to by the unauthorised website contacted. This is a shameful contravention of Article 16 of the DSA. Many of these complaints were to Viagogo, which claimed on the 22nd October 2025, "Viagogo is compliant in all markets in which it operates", despite our extensive evidence to the contrary.

evidence_quotes.quote: This complex and slow reporting process has cost our members time without achieving any successful removals of illegal content – the sole purpose of the DSA.

paragraphs.text: FEAT's members subsequently filed 20 complaints to Digital Services Coordinators (DSCs), of which five have been passed to the appropriate authorities. All five were processed months after the concerts had taken place, with no further action thus far – despite one of the reports dating back to July 2024. This complex and slow reporting process has cost our members time without achieving any successful removals of illegal content – the sole purpose of the DSA.

evidence_quotes.quote: with quicker takedowns
DSCs processing complaints more efficiently.

paragraphs.text: The DSA has the potential to act as an appropriate enforcement mechanism. It would, however, need to be vastly improved; with quicker takedowns, unauthorised websites held accountable for failure to respond to reports, and DSCs processing complaints more efficiently.

evidence_quotes.quote: illegal ticket resale

paragraphs.text: Include an assessment of illegal ticket resale as part of the DFA's planned 'Impact Assessment', conducting research across the issue to inform the wider debate
A step improvement in enforcement of EU and Member State legislation

evidence_quotes.quote: Updating the timeline for assessing and removing illegal content

paragraphs.text: Updating the timeline for assessing and removing illegal content

evidence_quotes.quote: categorically failing to respond to complaints of illegal content

paragraphs.text: Ticket resale websites are categorically failing to respond to complaints of illegal content, despite their legal obligation to do so (Article 16 of the DSA).

evidence_quotes.quote: rapid takedowns because of the time-sensitive nature of the content.
fraudulent tickets that appear before a concert has even gone 'on sale'.

paragraphs.text: The current requirement for online platforms to act "without undue delay" is far too vague and isn't working (as evidenced in FEAT's extensive reporting). In other areas, such as sports streaming piracy, the EU is increasingly recognising the need for rapid takedowns because of the time-sensitive nature of the content. Event ticketing is no exception, especially when considering fraudulent tickets that appear before a concert has even gone 'on sale' (read about an example of this here).

evidence_quotes.quote: higher sanctions for repeat offenders
geoblocking or takedowns of non-compliant online platforms

paragraphs.text: Establish clear criteria for penalties applied to breaches of the Digital Services Act, which are widely understood by online platforms. This could include higher sanctions for repeat offenders and/or geoblocking or takedowns of non-compliant online platforms.

Ensure national Digital Services Coordinators perform their duties effectively, guaranteeing that all Member States have a regulator that is 1) operational and 2) actively transmitting and investigating complaints within appropriate timeframes.

evidence_quotes.quote: annual sweeps of unauthorised resale websites

paragraphs.text: Strengthen cross-regulatory cooperation to guarantee that annual sweeps of unauthorised resale websites are conducted by the Consumer Protection Cooperation (CPC) network.
Encourage national consumer protection authorities to enforce court decisions that are obtained by event organisers.

evidence_quotes.quote: tickets resold at face value

paragraphs.text: Work with the live performance sector ahead of the publication of the DFA, so that event ticket resale quickly moves to a promoter-authorised only system, with tickets resold at face value

evidence_quotes.quote: total cost including booking fees, will always be made visible to consumers.

paragraphs.text: You will note that we are not calling for reforms such as more transparency around event ticket resale offers. This is because, once the process becomes authorised, the correct ticket information, including seat details (row, number), area, face-value price, and total cost including booking fees, will always be made visible to consumers.

In her mission letter to Commissioner Michael McGrath, President von der Leyen emphasised the need to protect vulnerable consumers, tackle challenges arising from e-commerce websites, and assess the need to update legislation and enforcement of existing consumer protection law.

section_path: Addressing illicit resale will deliver on the EU's targets for 2024-29

evidence_quotes.quote: The websites that publish fraudulent or misleading ticket offers exploit consumers and routinely ignore DSA takedown requests.

paragraphs.text: Unauthorised ticket resale sits at the heart of these objectives. The websites that publish fraudulent or misleading ticket offers exploit consumers and routinely ignore DSA takedown requests. These are clear examples of the unfair digital practices the Commission has pledged to eliminate. By improving enforcement and assessing the current legislative framework to ensure a system of promoter-authorised resale only, the EU would advance McGrath's priorities – ensuring online operators act responsibly and that consumers are protected when buying tickets.

section_path: Addressing illicit resale will deliver on the EU's targets for 2024-29

evidence_quotes.quote: further measures on ticket scalping
Strengthening enforcement of illegal ticket resale

paragraphs.text: The Commission's Digital Fairness Fitness Check (October 2024) confirmed broad support for action: 60% of respondents supported further measures on ticket scalping, with the report concluding that the EU must do more to address harmful practices that cause consumer detriment. Strengthening enforcement of illegal ticket resale would not only protect consumers but also deliver tangible progress on the Commission's priorities.

section_path: Addressing illicit resale will deliver on the EU's targets for 2024-29

paragraphs.text: By working with the sector and taking on board FEAT's recommendations, the EU can continue to set the gold standard for consumer protection.

section_path: Addressing illicit resale will deliver on the EU's targets for 2024-29

paragraphs.text: The Face-value European Alliance for Ticketing is a non-profit organisation, formed in 2019, to promote better ticket resale practices across Europe. FEAT's members are leading live event professionals from Austria, Belgium, Denmark, France, Germany, Ireland, the Netherlands, Spain, as well as EEA member Switzerland.
They have promoted for:
Music artists including Adele, Björk, Die Ärzte, Ed Sheeran, Elton John, Florence + the Machine, Iron Maiden, Radiohead, Rammstein and The Rolling Stones
Comedians including Ane Høgsberg, Aziz Ansari, Dara O'Brian, Maz Jobrani and Michael McIntyre
Festivals including Nova Rock Festival, OpenAir St Gallen, Summerdays Festival Arbon, Seaside Festival, Stars In Town, Unique Moments Zurich and Radar Festival Zurich
Venue members include famous Barcelona sites Estadi Olímpic and Palau Sant Jordi.
Sports members include major European football club FC Barcelona.
Supporting organisations include European live performance federation Pearle* – Live Performance Europe, European Music Managers Alliance, FanFair Alliance and campaign group Victim of Viagogo.
www.feat-alliance.org
The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
